explanation_md: `a + ", " + b` produces the string `"Hello, World"`. Counting the characters: `H-e-l-l-o-,-space-W-o-r-l-d` = 12 characters. `.length()` returns `12`, so the program prints `12`.

options.d: Nothing ŌĆö the program crashes because the name has a space.

explanation_md: `std::cin >>` reads one whitespace-delimited token. It stops at the first space, so only `"John"` is stored in `name`. `"Smith"` remains in the input buffer. To read both words as one string, `std::getline` would be needed instead.

prompt_md: Which header must you include to use `std::string`?
explanation_md: `std::string` is defined in the `<string>` header. While some compilers include it transitively through `<iostream>`, relying on that is not portable. Always include `<string>` explicitly when using `std::string`.

`word + word` concatenates `"cpp"` with itself to produce `"cppcpp"`. `.size()` returns `6` (the number of characters in `"cppcpp"`). The program prints `cppcpp` on the first line and `6` on the second.

options.a: No bug ŌĆö `std::cin >>` reads the entire line including spaces.
options.b: `std::cin >>` stops at the first whitespace, so only `Ada` is stored. The student should use `std::getline(std::cin, full_name)` instead.
options.c: The bug is that `std::string` cannot store names with spaces; use a `char` array instead.
options.d: The program will crash because `full_name` was not initialized to an empty string before reading.

explanation_md: `std::cin >>` reads a single whitespace-delimited token. With input `Ada Lovelace`, it stores only `"Ada"` and leaves `" Lovelace"` in the input buffer. The output would be `Name: Ada`, not the expected `Name: Ada Lovelace`. The fix is to use `std::getline(std::cin, full_name)`, which reads the entire line including spaces.
